corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: In figure 1, Adagrad outperforms SGD with Nesterov momentum by a large margin both with and without dropout noise.
Although our convergence analysis does not apply to non-convex problems, we empirically found that Adam often outperforms other methods in such cases.
Adam achieves the following guarantee, for all T ≥ 1.

The algorithm updates exponential moving averages of the gradient (m t ) and the squared gradient (v t ) where the hyper-parameters β 1 , β 2 ∈ [0, 1) control the exponential decay rates of these moving averages.

Note that the efficiency of algorithm 1 can, at the expense of clarity, be improved upon by changing the order of computation, e.g. by replacing the last three lines in the loop with the following lines:

The Sum-of-Functions Optimizer (SFO) is a quasi-Newton method based on minibatches, but (unlike Adam) has memory requirements linear in the number of minibatch partitions of a dataset, which is often infeasible on memory-constrained systems such as a GPU.
We evaluate our proposed method on L2-regularized multi-class logistic regression using the MNIST dataset.
We examine the sparse feature problem using IMDB movie review dataset from .

(left) Training cost for the first three epochs. (right) Training cost over 45 epochs.
See algorithm 1 for pseudo-code of our proposed algorithm Adam.
We introduce Adam, an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization of stochastic objective functions, based on adaptive estimates of lower-order moments.
We introduce Adam, an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization of stochastic objective functions, based on adaptive estimates of lower-order moments.

We also analyze the theoretical convergence properties of the algorithm and provide a regret bound on the convergence rate that is comparable to the best known results under the online convex optimization framework.

We also analyze the theoretical convergence properties of the algorithm and provide a regret bound on the convergence rate that is comparable to the best known results under the online convex optimization framework.
We propose Adam, a method for efficient stochastic optimization that only requires first-order gradients with little memory requirement.

Require: α: Stepsize Require: β 1 , β 2 ∈ [0, 1): Exponential decay rates for the moment estimates Require: f (θ): Stochastic objective function with parameters θ Require: In section 2 we describe the algorithm and the properties of its update rule.
Empirically, our method consistently outperforms other methods for a variety of models and datasets, as shown in section 6.
Overall, we show that Adam is a versatile algorithm that scales to large-scale high-dimensional machine learning problems.
We analyze the convergence of Adam using the online learning framework proposed in .

Since the nature of the sequence is unknown in advance, we evaluate our algorithm using the regret, that is the sum of all the previous difference between the online prediction f t (θ t ) and the best fixed point parameter f t (θ * ) from a feasible set X for all the previous steps.
